protocols.name: Snake Bite
protocols.summary: Rapid assessment, immobilization, and polyvalent anti-snake venom (ASV) administration for venomous snake envenomation.

timers.label: 20-min WBCT Check
timers.description: Whole blood clotting test — check at 20 min

timers.label: ASV Infusion Duration
timers.description: 10 vials over 1 hour IV

timers.label: Repeat WBCT
timers.description: Repeat clotting test every 6 hours

steps.text: Activate emergency — call for help immediately
steps.details: Alert senior physician, nurse, blood bank. Do NOT leave patient alone.

steps.text: Lay patient flat, immobilize bitten limb at heart level
steps.details: Splint the limb. Remove jewellery, rings, watches from affected limb. Do NOT elevate above heart level (increases venom absorption).

steps.text: Reassure patient — reduce movement and anxiety
steps.details: Fear increases heart rate and venom spread. Calm, reassuring approach.

steps.text: Do NOT: incise wound, suck venom, apply tourniquet, apply ice
steps.details: These actions cause harm. No tourniquets, no incisions, no herbal remedies.

steps.text: Establish IV access x2 (large bore), draw blood investigations
steps.details: Investigations: CBC, BT/CT, PT/INR/APTT, fibrinogen, RFT (BUN/Cr), urine dipstick, ECG.

steps.text: Perform 20-minute Whole Blood Clotting Test (WBCT)
steps.details: Place 2mL blood in clean glass tube. At 20 min: if blood is unclotted/partially clotted → hemotoxic envenomation confirmed. START WBCT TIMER.

steps.text: Assess for local envenomation signs
steps.details: Pain, swelling, bruising, blistering, necrosis at bite site. Measure and mark swelling extent with time stamps.

steps.text: Assess for systemic envenomation signs
steps.details: Neurological: ptosis, diplopia, dysphagia, respiratory weakness (neurotoxic — Krait/Cobra). Hemotoxic: bleeding gums, petechiae, hematuria, unclotted blood (Viper). Myotoxic: myalgia, myoglobinuria (Russell's Viper).

steps.text: If envenomation confirmed: pre-treat with Adrenaline 0.25mg SC
steps.details: Prophylactic subcutaneous adrenaline 0.25mg reduces ASV anaphylaxis risk. Give 5–10 minutes before ASV infusion.

steps.text: Administer Polyvalent ASV: 10 vials IV in 100mL Normal Saline over 1 hour
steps.details: Standard dose: 10 vials polyvalent ASV. Dilute in 100mL NS. Run over 60 minutes. Monitor for anaphylaxis. Have adrenaline ready. START ASV INFUSION TIMER.

steps.text: Monitor for ASV anaphylaxis reaction (first 30 min intensive monitoring)
steps.details: Signs: urticaria, bronchospasm, hypotension. If reaction: stop ASV, give adrenaline 0.5mg IM, restart ASV at slower rate after stabilization.

steps.text: For neurotoxic envenomation: give Neostigmine + Atropine test
steps.details: Atropine 0.6mg IV, then Neostigmine 0.5mg IV. If improvement in ptosis/strength within 30 min → continue every 30 min. (For Cobra bites especially).

steps.text: Repeat WBCT every 6 hours after ASV
steps.details: Persistent non-clotting at 6h: repeat ASV course (10 more vials). Target: clotting restored within 6 hours.

steps.text: Tetanus toxoid 0.5mL IM + wound care
steps.details: If not immunized: also give Tetanus immunoglobulin 250U IM. Gentle wound cleaning with antiseptic.

steps.text: Strict urine output monitoring (catheterize), monitor for AKI
steps.details: Target urine output >0.5mL/kg/h. Russell's Viper bite: high risk of acute kidney injury. IV fluids for oliguria.

medications.name: Polyvalent Anti-Snake Venom (ASV)
medications.dose: 10 vials IV in 100mL NS over 60 min
medications.note: Bharat Serums / VINS polyvalent ASV (covers Cobra, Krait, Russell's Viper, Saw-scaled Viper)

medications.name: Adrenaline (Epinephrine) 1:1000
medications.note: Prophylaxis against ASV anaphylaxis

medications.name: Atropine
medications.note: For Neostigmine test (cholinergic blockade before Neostigmine)

medications.name: Neostigmine
medications.note: For neurotoxic snakes — Cobra/Krait (test response first)

medications.name: Chlorpheniramine
medications.note: H1 blocker — prophylaxis with ASV

medications.name: Tetanus Toxoid
medications.note: If not recently immunized

red_flags: Bilateral ptosis, difficulty swallowing or breathing (neurotoxic — imminent respiratory failure)
Active bleeding from gums, nose, IV sites (hemotoxic coagulopathy)
Swelling reaching or crossing a joint above the bite within 2 hours
Urine output <0.5mL/kg/h (AKI — Russell's Viper)
Shock, hypotension, altered consciousness
Dark urine (myoglobinuria or hemoglobinuria)
pre_referral: Secure airway — jaw support/NPA if ptosis and breathing difficulty
At least 10 vials ASV given (if not previously given)
Blood samples sent to referral center
Document time of bite, time of first ASV, response to ASV
referral_indications: Respiratory failure / declining SpO2 despite oxygen
Persistent coagulopathy after 2 courses of ASV (20 vials)
Massive local necrosis requiring surgical debridement

protocols.name: Acute MI
protocols.summary: STEMI/NSTEMI protocol: rapid reperfusion via thrombolysis or PCI, antiplatelet and anticoagulation therapy.

timers.label: Door-to-ECG Target
timers.description: 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of arrival

timers.label: Streptokinase Infusion
timers.description: 1.5 MU IV over 60 minutes

timers.label: Thrombolysis Window
timers.description: Symptom onset to SK: ideally <3h, maximum 12h

steps.text: MONA: Morphine, Oxygen, Nitrates, Aspirin — assess simultaneously
steps.details: Establish IV access immediately. Monitor: continuous ECG, BP, SpO2, pulse oximetry.

steps.text: Perform 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes — interpret STAT
steps.details: Look for: ST elevation ≥1mm in 2 contiguous leads (STEMI), new LBBB, ST depression (NSTEMI). START ECG TIMER.

steps.text: Aspirin 325mg — chew and swallow (if no allergy or active GI bleed)
steps.details: Chewing gives faster absorption. This is the single most important medication.

steps.text: Clopidogrel 300mg PO loading dose (or Ticagrelor 180mg if available)
steps.details: Dual antiplatelet therapy. Give within 30 min of diagnosis.

steps.text: Oxygen 2-4L/min if SpO2 < 94%. Do NOT give oxygen if SpO2 ≥94%
steps.details: Hyperoxia is harmful in STEMI. Target SpO2 94-98%.

steps.text: Sublingual Nitroglycerin 0.4mg — if BP >90/60 and no inferior/right MI
steps.details: Contraindicated in: suspected right ventricular MI (inferior STEMI), systolic BP <90, recent phosphodiesterase inhibitor use within 24-48h.

steps.text: Draw bloods: Troponin, CBC, RFT, electrolytes, lipid profile, coagulation
steps.details: Troponin may be normal in first 4-6 hours. Serial troponin at 0 and 3-6 hours.

steps.text: LMWH (Enoxaparin) 1mg/kg SC — or UFH 60U/kg IV bolus (max 4000U)
steps.details: Anticoagulation reduces further thrombosis. Enoxaparin preferred if no PCI planned. Reduce dose in CKD (eGFR <30).

dose_calc.note: Do not exceed 100mg. Reduce to 0.5mg/kg if eGFR<30 or age>75

steps.text: Atorvastatin 80mg PO stat (high-intensity statin)
steps.details: Start immediately. Continue long-term. Do not delay for fasting lipid profile.

steps.text: IF STEMI: Decision — PCI or Thrombolysis
steps.details: PCI preferred if door-to-balloon ≤90 min (≤120 min for transfer). If PCI not available within 120 min → give Streptokinase.

steps.text: Streptokinase 1.5 MU in 100mL NS IV over 60 minutes (if PCI not available)
steps.details: Contraindications: prior intracranial bleed, hemorrhagic stroke <3 months, BP >180/110, active major bleeding, prior SK within 6 months. START SK INFUSION TIMER.

steps.text: Post-SK monitoring: ECG at 60-90 min, look for ST resolution >50%
steps.details: Resolution of ST elevation >50% + pain relief = reperfusion success. Reperfusion arrhythmia (accelerated idioventricular rhythm) = normal finding.

steps.text: IV Beta-blocker if tachycardia/hypertension (and no contraindications)
steps.details: Metoprolol 5mg IV slowly q5min x3, then oral. Contraindicated: HR<60, BP<100, AV block, severe bronchospasm, acute HF.

steps.text: Transfer to CCU, insert urinary catheter, NPO initially
steps.details: Continuous cardiac monitoring. Defibrillator available. IV access maintained.

medications.name: Aspirin
medications.note: First and most critical drug

medications.name: Clopidogrel
medications.note: Or Ticagrelor 180mg loading if available

medications.name: Streptokinase
medications.dose: 1.5 MU in 100mL NS IV over 60 min
medications.note: For STEMI when PCI unavailable. Check contraindications.

medications.name: Enoxaparin
medications.note: Reduce dose if CKD. Hold if surgical intervention planned

medications.name: Atorvastatin
medications.note: High-intensity statin. Start immediately.

medications.name: Nitroglycerin
medications.dose: 0.4mg SL q5min x3, then IV infusion 10-200mcg/min
medications.note: Avoid if right MI, hypotension, phosphodiesterase inhibitors

medications.name: Metoprolol
medications.dose: 5mg IV q5min x3 then 25-50mg PO BD
medications.note: Avoid if HR<60, BP<100, AV block, severe bronchospasm

red_flags: Cardiogenic shock: SBP <90, cold extremities, altered consciousness
Acute pulmonary edema (use APE protocol)
Complete heart block (bradycardia, wide complex escape rhythm)
Ventricular fibrillation (use cardiac arrest protocol)
ST re-elevation after initial resolution (re-occlusion)
Failure of SK reperfusion: ST not resolved by 50% at 90 min
pre_referral: Streptokinase given if STEMI and PCI delay >120 min
IV access x2, defibrillator available for transfer
referral_indications: All STEMI patients for PCI evaluation
NSTEMI with high-risk features (recurrent ischemia, hemodynamic instability)

protocols.name: Acute Stroke
protocols.summary: Rapid stroke recognition (FAST), neuroimaging, and thrombolysis within 4.5-hour window.

timers.label: Thrombolysis Window
timers.description: Alteplase eligible within 4.5h of last known well

timers.label: Door-to-Needle Target
timers.description: IV tPA within 60 minutes of arrival

timers.label: BP Recheck After Treatment
timers.description: Recheck BP every 15 min for 2h after tPA

steps.text: FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call
steps.details: Note exact time of symptom onset or 'last known well'. This is critical for thrombolysis eligibility.

steps.text: Airway management — if GCS <8 or unable to protect airway: intubate
steps.details: Position head 15-30 degrees up. Supplemental oxygen only if SpO2 <94%.

steps.text: Check blood glucose immediately
steps.details: Hypoglycemia (BG <60 mg/dL) mimics stroke — treat with 25-50mL 50% dextrose IV. Hyperglycemia (>200) worsens outcome — treat with insulin.

steps.text: Perform NIHSS (NIH Stroke Scale) scoring
steps.details: NIHSS assesses: consciousness (0-3), gaze (0-2), visual fields (0-3), facial palsy (0-3), arm motor (0-4 each), leg motor (0-4 each), limb ataxia, sensation, best language, dysarthria, extinction (0-2). Total 0-42.

steps.text: Non-contrast CT head STAT — rule out hemorrhage before thrombolysis
steps.details: CT is mandatory before thrombolysis. Hemorrhagic stroke = absolute contraindication to tPA. ASPECTS score if available.

steps.text: Blood tests: CBC, INR/PT/APTT, glucose, electrolytes, renal function
steps.details: Do NOT delay tPA for bloods unless coagulopathy suspected.

steps.text: Blood pressure management — do NOT routinely treat unless extremely high
steps.details: If no tPA: treat only if BP >220/120. If giving tPA: target <185/110 before and during infusion. Use Labetalol 10mg IV or Nicardipine infusion.

steps.text: IF ischemic + within 4.5h + no contraindications: Alteplase 0.9mg/kg IV
steps.details: Alteplase: 10% as IV bolus over 1 min, remaining 90% as infusion over 60 min. Max dose 90mg. Do NOT give antiplatelet/anticoagulant for 24h after tPA.

dose_calc.note: 10% as bolus, 90% over 60 min. Max 90mg total

steps.text: Aspirin 300mg (if NO hemorrhage + NOT receiving tPA in next 24h)
steps.details: Aspirin alone if tPA not given. Do NOT give within 24h of tPA.

steps.text: Temperature management: target normothermia (paracetamol if febrile)
steps.details: Fever worsens outcome. Antipyretics if temp >37.5°C.

steps.text: Post-tPA: BP every 15 min for 2h, then 30 min for 6h, then 1h for 16h
steps.details: Watch for symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage: sudden deterioration, headache, BP spike. If suspected: STOP infusion, urgent CT.

steps.text: Nil by mouth until formal swallow assessment
steps.details: Aspiration pneumonia is a leading cause of post-stroke death. Formal dysphagia screen before any oral intake.

medications.name: Alteplase (rt-PA)
medications.note: Only if ischemic stroke within 4.5h, CT cleared, no contraindications

medications.name: Aspirin
medications.dose: 300mg PO or NGT stat (if not receiving tPA)
medications.note: Do NOT give within 24h of alteplase

medications.name: Labetalol
medications.dose: 10-20mg IV over 1-2 min (repeat q10-20 min, max 300mg)
medications.note: For BP management if >220/120 (no tPA) or >185/110 (pre-tPA)

medications.name: Paracetamol
medications.note: For temperature management if febrile

medications.name: 50% Dextrose
medications.note: Hypoglycemia must be treated urgently — it mimics stroke

red_flags: Declining GCS — airway compromise imminent
BP not controllable below 185/110 pre-tPA (do not give tPA)
Post-tPA deterioration (stop infusion, urgent CT for hemorrhage)
Seizures (may indicate hemorrhagic transformation)
Signs of herniation: unequal pupils, Cushing's triad
pre_referral: CT head done (confirm ischemic or hemorrhagic)
Time of last known well documented clearly
referral_indications: Any stroke patient for neuroimaging (if not available locally)
Candidate for thrombectomy (large vessel occlusion, NIHSS >6, within 24h)

protocols.name: Anaphylaxis
protocols.summary: Life-threatening hypersensitivity reaction. Epinephrine IM is the cornerstone of treatment — give FIRST, other measures follow.

timers.label: Repeat Epinephrine
timers.description: If no improvement: repeat IM epinephrine after 5-15 min

timers.label: Minimum Observation
timers.description: Observe for minimum 4h (8h if severe)

steps.text: Intramuscular Epinephrine (Adrenaline) 0.01mg/kg in outer thigh — FIRST LINE
steps.details: Use 1:1000 (1mg/mL) adrenaline. Outer mid-thigh IM. This is the ONLY first-line treatment. Do not delay for IV access.

steps.text: Call for help — activate emergency team
steps.details: Anaphylaxis can be fatal within minutes. Immediate team activation required.

steps.text: Position patient appropriately
steps.details: If conscious and breathing: lie flat with legs elevated. If breathing difficulty: semi-recumbent position. Do NOT stand or sit upright suddenly (risk of cardiovascular collapse).

steps.text: High-flow oxygen 15L/min via non-rebreather mask
steps.details: Target SpO2 >95%. Consider bag-mask ventilation if inadequate breathing.

steps.text: Large bore IV x2 — IV fluid resuscitation
steps.details: Normal Saline 500mL-1L rapid IV bolus (20mL/kg in children). Repeat if hypotension persists.

steps.text: If NO improvement after 5-15 min: REPEAT IM Epinephrine
steps.details: Up to 3 IM doses of epinephrine may be given. If still no response → IV epinephrine infusion in ICU. START TIMER.

steps.text: Antihistamine: Chlorpheniramine 10mg IV (adult)
steps.details: Secondary treatment only — does NOT replace epinephrine. Child: 0.2mg/kg IV.

dose_calc.note: Adult: 10mg IV. Child: 0.2mg/kg. Give slowly over 5 min

steps.text: Corticosteroid: Hydrocortisone 200mg IV stat (adult)
steps.details: Prevents/reduces biphasic reaction (occurs in 20%). Not immediate effect. Child: 4mg/kg IV.

steps.text: Bronchospasm: Nebulized Salbutamol 2.5mg (child) / 5mg (adult)
steps.details: If wheeze persists after epinephrine. Second dose may be given.

steps.text: If on Beta-blockers with inadequate response: Glucagon 1-2mg IV
steps.details: Beta-blockers block epinephrine effects. Glucagon 1-2mg IV over 5 min, then infusion 5-15mcg/min.

steps.text: Monitor: BP q5min, SpO2 continuous, ECG, urine output
steps.details: Biphasic anaphylaxis occurs in up to 20% — symptoms return 1-8 hours after initial resolution.

steps.text: Observe for minimum 4 hours (8 hours if severe reaction)
steps.details: All patients must be observed for biphasic reaction. Discharge criteria: stable BP, SpO2 >95%, no ongoing symptoms.

steps.text: Prescribe epinephrine auto-injector on discharge + allergy alert
steps.details: Refer to allergist. Document trigger. Advise MedAlert bracelet.

medications.name: Epinephrine (Adrenaline) 1:1000
medications.note: FIRST AND ONLY first-line drug. Repeat every 5-15 min if no improvement.

medications.name: Normal Saline
medications.note: Rapid infusion for hypotension. Repeat as needed.

medications.name: Chlorpheniramine
medications.note: Secondary treatment only — not first-line

medications.name: Hydrocortisone
medications.note: Prevents biphasic reaction. Not immediate effect.

medications.name: Salbutamol (Albuterol)
medications.note: For bronchospasm/wheeze refractory to epinephrine

medications.name: Glucagon
medications.note: Only if on beta-blockers and inadequate epinephrine response

red_flags: No improvement after 2 doses of IM epinephrine (needs IV epinephrine in ICU)
Stridor, severe bronchospasm — impending airway loss
Unresponsive to treatment, worsening shock
Cardiac arrest (initiate BLS/ACLS)
Pregnancy (fetal compromise — epinephrine safe, do not withhold)
pre_referral: At least 1 dose IM epinephrine given
IV access established with fluid running
referral_indications: Refractory anaphylaxis (>2 doses epinephrine with no response)

protocols.name: Status Epilepticus
protocols.summary: Seizure lasting >5 minutes or ≥2 seizures without return to baseline. Benzodiazepine escalation to Phenytoin/Valproate.

timers.label: First Benzo Response Time
timers.description: If still seizing 5 min after first dose → repeat benzo

timers.label: Phenytoin Loading
timers.description: 20mg/kg over 20 min (max 50mg/min) with ECG monitoring

timers.label: Total Treatment Window
timers.description: Target seizure cessation within 20-30 min

steps.text: Note exact time — SE = seizure >5 min or 2+ seizures without recovery
steps.details: Document time seizure started. Risk of neuronal injury increases significantly beyond 30-60 min.

steps.text: Airway: positioning, suction, NPA if needed. Oxygen 10-15L via mask
steps.details: Lateral position (recovery position) to prevent aspiration. Do NOT put fingers in mouth. NPA if needed — do NOT intubate initially unless specifically indicated.

steps.text: Check blood glucose STAT (finger prick)
steps.details: Hypoglycemia is a common, treatable cause. If <60 mg/dL: give 50mL 50% dextrose IV (adult) or 2-4mL/kg 25% dextrose (child) over 5 min.

steps.text: IV access — draw blood for investigations simultaneously
steps.details: Investigations: glucose, electrolytes (Na, K, Ca, Mg), RFT, LFT, toxicology screen, drug levels (phenytoin if on it), CBC.

steps.text: First-line (0-5 min): Lorazepam 0.1mg/kg IV at 2mg/min (or Diazepam 0.2mg/kg IV)
steps.details: If no IV: Midazolam 0.2mg/kg IM/buccal (highly effective). Diazepam 0.5mg/kg PR if neither available. START TIMER for 5 min response check.

dose_calc.note: Adult: 4mg IV. Child: 0.1mg/kg (max 4mg). Preferred over Diazepam

steps.text: If still seizing at 5 min: REPEAT benzodiazepine once
steps.details: One more dose of same benzodiazepine (same dose). If IV not available: Midazolam 10mg IM (adult).

steps.text: Second-line (10 min): Phenytoin 20mg/kg IV loading dose
steps.details: Dilute in Normal Saline (NOT dextrose). Rate: MAX 50mg/min in adults, 1mg/kg/min in children. ECG monitoring mandatory during infusion (cardiac monitoring). START PHENYTOIN TIMER.

dose_calc.note: Max rate 50mg/min (adult). Use NS — incompatible with dextrose. Monitor BP and ECG.

steps.text: Alternative second-line: Valproate 40mg/kg IV (preferred in children)
steps.details: Sodium Valproate 40mg/kg IV over 10-15 min. Safe alternative to Phenytoin, especially in children. Avoid in liver disease, pregnancy.

dose_calc.note: Preferred in children. Avoid in liver disease, pregnant women, children <2y

steps.text: Thiamine 100mg IV before glucose — if alcoholism suspected
steps.details: Prevents Wernicke's encephalopathy in alcoholic patients.

steps.text: Refractory SE (>20-30 min): Intubate, ICU transfer, anesthetic agent
steps.details: Propofol infusion 1-2mg/kg then 2-10mg/kg/h OR Midazolam infusion 0.05-0.2mg/kg/h. Continuous EEG monitoring in ICU.

medications.name: Lorazepam
medications.note: First-line preferred. Longer action than Diazepam

medications.name: Diazepam
medications.note: Alternative first-line. Rectal route if no IV

medications.name: Midazolam
medications.note: Highly effective when no IV access. Buccal Midazolam 5mg (child)

medications.name: Phenytoin
medications.note: ECG monitoring mandatory. In NS only — NOT dextrose. Max 1500mg.

medications.name: Levetiracetam
medications.note: Alternative to Phenytoin; safer cardiac profile. Available as 500mg vials.

medications.name: Sodium Valproate
medications.note: Preferred in children. Avoid in pregnancy and liver disease.

medications.name: 50% Dextrose
medications.note: Always check glucose first. Give after Thiamine if alcoholism possible

red_flags: SE >30 min (super-refractory) — cerebral damage risk, needs ICU
Hypoxia (SpO2 <90%) — consider intubation
Post-ictal hemiplegia or focal deficit (Todd's paralysis vs. acute structural lesion)
New-onset SE in febrile child >1 month (consider meningitis, encephalitis)
SE despite 3 medications — needs anaesthetic
Glucose <40 mg/dL
pre_referral: At least 2 antiepileptic drugs given in sequence
Document all medications given with times and doses
Check electrolytes: Na, Ca, Mg abnormality may be cause
referral_indications: Refractory SE (>20-30 min, not responding to 2 antiepileptics)
Suspected CNS infection (fever, stiff neck) — lumbar puncture needed
First-ever SE in adult without known epilepsy

protocols.name: Obstetric Emergency
protocols.summary: Covers Postpartum Hemorrhage (PPH) and Eclampsia — the two leading causes of maternal mortality in India.

timers.label: MgSO4 Loading Dose Duration
timers.description: 4g MgSO4 IV over 20 minutes for eclampsia

timers.label: Uterotonics Reassessment
timers.description: Reassess PPH response at 15 min after uterotonics

steps.text: CALL: Activate obstetric emergency team (obstetrician, anesthetist, blood bank, neonatologist)
steps.details: Clear communication: state 'Obstetric Emergency — PPH/Eclampsia' with location.

steps.text: ASSESS: Vital signs — BP, HR, SpO2, estimated blood loss
steps.details: Postpartum hemorrhage = blood loss >500mL after vaginal delivery or >1000mL after C-section. Underestimation is common — double your estimated loss.

steps.text: PPH — Step 1: IV access x2 (large bore), aggressive fluid resuscitation
steps.details: 1-2L NS or Hartmann's rapid IV. Send blood for CBC, coagulation, type and cross-match. Foley catheter. Oxygen 15L/min.

steps.text: PPH — Step 2: Bimanual uterine massage + uterine compression
steps.details: Massage the uterine fundus immediately. If uterus is soft/boggy (uterine atony — 80% of PPH): massage continuously while preparing uterotonics.

steps.text: PPH — Step 3: Oxytocin 10 IU IM stat + infusion 20 IU in 500mL NS at 60 drops/min
steps.details: First-line uterotonic. IM injection at same time as IV infusion. Do not give as IV bolus — causes hypotension.

steps.text: PPH — Step 4: Misoprostol 800mcg sublingual/rectal (if Oxytocin unavailable or insufficient)
steps.details: Where Oxytocin is unavailable (e.g., no cold chain), Misoprostol 800mcg is life-saving. Also add Carbetocin 100mcg IM if available.

steps.text: PPH — Step 5: Tranexamic acid 1g IV over 10 min (within 3 hours of delivery)
steps.details: Anti-fibrinolytic — reduces PPH mortality by 30% (WHO WOMAN trial). Can repeat 1g in 30 min if bleeding continues. Must be given within 3 hours.

steps.text: PPH — Step 6: Ergometrine 0.5mg IM (if no hypertension, asthma, or cardiac disease)
steps.details: Contraindicated in hypertension (causes severe BP rise). Causes sustained uterine contraction. IM route preferred.

steps.text: ECLAMPSIA — Step 1: Magnesium Sulfate (MgSO4) loading dose
steps.details: MgSO4 4g IV in 200mL NS over 20 min. Monitor: respiratory rate (must be >16/min), deep tendon reflexes (must be present), urine output (>30mL/h). START MgSO4 TIMER.

dose_calc.note: 50% MgSO4 = 500mg/mL. 4g = 8mL. Dilute to 200mL. Antidote: Calcium gluconate 1g IV

steps.text: ECLAMPSIA — Step 2: MgSO4 maintenance infusion 1-2g/h IV
steps.details: Continue for 24h after last seizure. Monitor every 15-30 min: RR, DTR, urine output. If toxicity signs: STOP MgSO4, give Calcium Gluconate 1g IV.

steps.text: ECLAMPSIA — Step 3: Antihypertensives if BP >160/110
steps.details: Hydralazine 5mg IV over 5 min (repeat q20min, max 20mg). OR Labetalol 20mg IV (double dose q10min, max 300mg). OR Nifedipine 10mg PO (may repeat once).

steps.text: Prepare for delivery — discuss with obstetrician for timing
steps.details: Delivery is the definitive treatment for eclampsia. Steroid 12mg IM (betamethasone) if <34 weeks gestation for fetal lung maturity.

steps.text: Blood transfusion: O-negative if urgent, type-matched as available
steps.details: Transfuse if: Hb <7g/dL, ongoing major hemorrhage, symptomatic anemia. FFP and platelets if coagulopathy.

medications.name: Oxytocin
medications.dose: 10 IU IM stat + 20 IU in 500mL NS at 60 dpm
medications.note: First-line for PPH. Never IV bolus.

medications.name: Misoprostol
medications.note: Where Oxytocin unavailable (cold chain issues). Also WHO option.

medications.name: Tranexamic Acid
medications.dose: 1g IV over 10 min; repeat once if needed
medications.note: Must give within 3 hours. Reduces maternal mortality (WOMAN trial)

medications.name: Ergometrine
medications.note: Contraindicated in hypertension, pre-eclampsia, cardiac disease

medications.name: MgSO4 (50% solution)
medications.dose: 4g IV loading over 20 min, then 1-2g/h infusion
medications.note: For eclampsia. Antidote: Calcium gluconate 1g IV if RR<16 or absent DTR

medications.name: Hydralazine
medications.note: For severe hypertension in eclampsia

medications.name: Calcium Gluconate
medications.note: MgSO4 ANTIDOTE — give if respiratory depression or absent reflexes

red_flags: PPH >1000mL with continued bleeding (prepare for surgical intervention)
Hemodynamic instability despite 2L fluid replacement (transfuse)
MgSO4 toxicity: RR <16/min, absent deep tendon reflexes, SpO2 dropping
Eclamptic seizures not controlled after 2 doses MgSO4
BP >180/120 refractory to antihypertensives (hypertensive emergency)
Fetal bradycardia <100bpm (emergency delivery consideration)
pre_referral: MgSO4 loading dose given for eclampsia
Document all medications, timing, and blood loss volume
referral_indications: PPH >1000mL not responding to medical management (surgical options)

protocols.name: Pediatric Emergency
protocols.summary: Covers neonatal resuscitation, pediatric septic shock, febrile seizures, and acute respiratory distress in children.

timers.label: NRP Golden Minute
timers.description: Complete initial steps within 60 seconds of birth

timers.label: Febrile Seizure Benzo Response
timers.description: If seizure >5 min or no response at 5 min — repeat benzo

steps.text: NEONATAL RESUSCITATION (NRP): Warm, Dry, Stimulate — within 60 seconds
steps.details: Golden minute: Warm (radiant warmer), position airway (sniffing position), dry and stimulate. Assess: breathing (regular?), heart rate (>100?), colour (central cyanosis?). START GOLDEN MINUTE TIMER.

steps.text: NRP: If not breathing or HR<100: Positive Pressure Ventilation (PPV)
steps.details: BVM at 40-60 breaths/min. FiO2 21% (room air) for term neonates. FiO2 30% for preterm. Check chest rise. If HR remains <60 after 30s: start chest compressions.

steps.text: NRP: HR <60 after PPV: Chest compressions (3:1 ratio with breaths)
steps.details: Rate 90 compressions + 30 breaths per minute. Depth: 1/3 of AP chest diameter. Use 2-thumb encircling technique.

steps.text: PEDIATRIC SEPTIC SHOCK: Recognize early — 'warm shock' easily missed in children
steps.details: Signs: fever + tachycardia + altered perfusion. 'Cold shock': mottling, prolonged cap refill >3s, weak pulses. 'Warm shock': flushed, bounding pulses, wide pulse pressure.

steps.text: Septic Shock — IV/IO access: Fluid bolus 10-20mL/kg NS over 5-10 min
steps.details: CAUTION: FEAST trial shows excess fluids worsen mortality in African children. Start 10mL/kg, reassess after each bolus. Max 40-60mL/kg in first hour.

dose_calc.note: 10mL/kg per bolus. Reassess after each. Max 40-60mL/kg first hour

steps.text: Septic Shock — Antibiotics within 1 hour: Ceftriaxone 100mg/kg IV stat
steps.details: Neonates (<28 days): Ampicillin 50mg/kg IV + Gentamicin 4-7mg/kg IV. Children: Ceftriaxone 100mg/kg IV (max 2g) OR Cefotaxime 150mg/kg/day IV divided q6h.

steps.text: FEBRILE SEIZURE: Assess — is this simple or complex?
steps.details: Simple: generalized, <15 min, one seizure per episode, no postictal deficit. Complex: focal, >15 min, recurrent within 24h, or Todd's paralysis. Complex = higher risk of meningitis/encephalitis.

steps.text: Febrile Seizure >5 min: Diazepam 0.5mg/kg rectal OR Midazolam 0.2mg/kg buccal/IM
steps.details: Do not give IV benzodiazepine without IV access. Buccal Midazolam or rectal Diazepam are equally effective and faster to give. START RESPONSE TIMER.

steps.text: Hypoglycemia in children: Dextrose 10% 5mL/kg IV bolus
steps.details: Use 10% dextrose for children (not 50% — causes hyperosmolarity). Target glucose 80-140 mg/dL.

dose_calc.note: Do NOT use 50% dextrose in children. Use 10% dextrose 5mL/kg.

steps.text: Croup (stridor at rest): Adrenaline nebulization + Dexamethasone
steps.details: Nebulized Adrenaline 0.5mL/kg (max 5mL) of 1:1000 solution. Dexamethasone 0.15-0.6mg/kg IM/PO/IV (max 10mg). Monitor for rebound stridor at 2 hours.

steps.text: Paediatric vital sign interpretation — know normal ranges
steps.details: Normal HR: Newborn 120-160; Infant 80-140; Toddler 80-130; Pre-school 80-120; School-age 75-115; Adolescent 60-100. Normal RR: Newborn 40-60; Infant 30-40; School 20-30; Adolescent 15-20. Low BP criteria: SBP <70+(2xage in years) mmHg

medications.name: Ceftriaxone (Sepsis)
medications.note: Neonates <28 days: Ampicillin + Gentamicin instead

medications.name: Dextrose 10% (Hypoglycemia)
medications.note: Target glucose 80-140. Repeat if needed. Do NOT use 50% dextrose in children.

medications.name: Midazolam (Seizure)
medications.note: Buccal route highly effective. Also: Diazepam 0.5mg/kg rectal

medications.name: Diazepam
medications.note: Rectal route when no IV access

medications.name: Adrenaline nebulization (Croup)
medications.note: L-epinephrine preferred but regular 1:1000 works

medications.name: Dexamethasone (Croup)
medications.note: Single dose is highly effective. PO as effective as IV.

red_flags: Neonatal HR <60 despite 30s effective PPV (cardiac arrest — chest compressions)
Meningococcal rash: non-blanching petechiae/purpura with fever
Bulging fontanelle (raised ICP — do NOT lumbar puncture until imaging)
Respiratory failure in child: paradoxical breathing, silent chest
Septic shock not responding to 40-60mL/kg IV fluids + antibiotics
Altered consciousness in febrile child (suspect encephalitis/meningitis)
pre_referral: Seizures controlled (at least one benzodiazepine dose given)
referral_indications: Neonatal sepsis in any neonate <28 days

protocols.name: Acute Pulmonary Edema
protocols.summary: Flash pulmonary edema: maximize sitting position, oxygen, diuresis, vasodilation. Identify and treat underlying cause.

timers.label: Furosemide Response Check
timers.description: Assess urine output at 60 min after IV Furosemide

steps.text: POSITION: Sit patient upright with legs dangling off bed (cardiac position)
steps.details: Gravity reduces venous return and preload. This positioning alone can improve symptoms significantly.

steps.text: High-flow oxygen: 10-15L/min via non-rebreather mask (target SpO2 >95%)
steps.details: If SpO2 <90% or worsening despite O2: CPAP/BiPAP is superior to intubation (reduces intubation rates by 50%). If no NIV: bag-mask ventilation.

steps.text: IV access x2, 12-lead ECG, check glucose, CXR (portable)
steps.details: ECG: looking for STEMI (immediate reperfusion), new AF/flutter (rate control), arrhythmia. CXR: bat-wing shadowing, kerley B lines, cardiomegaly.

steps.text: IV Furosemide 40-80mg IV bolus (or double home oral dose)
steps.details: START RESPONSE TIMER for urine output at 60 min. Onset of vasodilation: 5 min. Diuresis: 30-60 min. If no response: repeat dose or escalate.

steps.text: Sublingual Nitroglycerin 0.4mg — if SBP >100 mmHg
steps.details: Reduces preload and afterload. Can repeat q5min x3, then IV infusion 10-200mcg/min. Avoid if SBP <100, recent PDE5 inhibitor, RV infarction.

steps.text: If STEMI identified on ECG — activate cardiac catheterization or give SK (see Acute MI protocol)
steps.details: APE in context of STEMI = cardiogenic shock risk. Immediate reperfusion is the priority.

steps.text: CPAP/NIV: if available and patient cooperative (SpO2 <90% despite O2)
steps.details: CPAP 5-10cmH2O significantly reduces need for intubation. BiPAP: IPAP 10-15, EPAP 5.

steps.text: Morphine 2-4mg IV slowly — use with caution (controversial in APE)
steps.details: Reduces anxiety and preload. Associated with increased mortality in some studies. Use only if severe agitation and respiratory distress, and only small doses.

steps.text: Identify and treat underlying cause
steps.details: Common causes: Acute MI, hypertensive crisis, arrhythmia (AF with fast ventricular response), acute valvular dysfunction, non-compliance with diuretics.

steps.text: Monitor: strict urine output, daily weight, electrolytes (K+ loss with furosemide)
steps.details: Target urine output >0.5mL/kg/h. Watch for hypokalemia (K+ <3.5) — may precipitate arrhythmias.

medications.name: Furosemide (Frusemide)
medications.dose: 40-80mg IV bolus; repeat if no diuresis in 1 hour
medications.note: High-dose may be needed if on chronic diuretics. Monitor K+

medications.name: Nitroglycerin (GTN)
medications.note: Avoid if SBP<100, RV MI, or recent PDE5 inhibitor

medications.name: Morphine
medications.note: Use cautiously — some evidence of harm. Only if severe distress

medications.name: Potassium Chloride (KCl)
medications.note: Furosemide causes K+ depletion. Monitor electrolytes.

red_flags: SpO2 <90% despite maximal oxygen (NIV or intubation)
BP <90 systolic (cardiogenic shock — needs ICU, vasopressors)
STEMI on ECG (immediate reperfusion)
Rapid AF rate >150 with hemodynamic compromise (cardioversion)
Progressive respiratory failure despite CPAP
referral_indications: Need for ICU and mechanical ventilation

protocols.name: Poisoning
protocols.summary: General poisoning management plus specific OPC (Organophosphate) protocol — the most common fatal poisoning in rural India.

timers.label: Atropine Re-dose Interval
timers.description: Repeat Atropine every 5-10 min until secretions dry

timers.label: Pralidoxime Loading
timers.description: 1-2g IV over 30 minutes

steps.text: PERSONNEL SAFETY: Use gloves, gown, eye protection for OPC decontamination
steps.details: Organophosphate poisoning: secondary contamination of healthcare workers is REAL. Wear double gloves, gown, eye protection before handling patient.

steps.text: Remove patient from exposure; remove all contaminated clothing outdoors
steps.details: Cut off contaminated clothing. Wash skin with soap and water x3. Flush eyes with water for 15 min if eye exposure.

steps.text: ABCDE assessment — secure airway (vomiting/secretions common)
steps.details: OPC causes profuse secretions (SLUDGE). May need suction, NPA, or intubation. Assess GCS, pupils (miosis = OPC), HR, BP.

steps.text: Identify toxin if possible: empty containers, history from family
steps.details: Common poisons in India: Organophosphates (pesticides: Chlorpyrifos, Monocrotophos), Carbamates, Oleander (Kaneru), Datura, Aluminum phosphide (grain preservative — NO antidote).

steps.text: IV access x2, blood investigations: RFT, LFT, glucose, CBC, ABG, cholinesterase level if available
steps.details: Cholinesterase (RBC and plasma) confirms OPC poisoning. ABG assesses respiratory failure.

steps.text: GENERAL: Activated charcoal 1g/kg (50g adult) if within 1-2h, airway protected, not corrosive
steps.details: Contraindicated: corrosives (acids/alkalis), hydrocarbons, obtunded patient without secured airway, Aluminium phosphide.

steps.text: OPC SPECIFIC: Atropine 2-4mg IV immediately (titrate to effect)
steps.details: Titrate to: dry secretions (no bronchospasm, no wheeze). NOT to HR or dry skin. Repeat every 5-10 min. May require 10-100mg+ in severe cases. START ATROPINE INTERVAL TIMER.

dose_calc.note: Start with 2mg IV. Double dose every 5-10 min until secretions dry. No fixed ceiling dose.

steps.text: OPC SPECIFIC: Pralidoxime (2-PAM) 1-2g IV over 30 min (within 24h of exposure)
steps.details: Works only if started within 24h (before 'aging' of cholinesterase). Load 1-2g IV over 30 min, then maintenance 250-500mg/h. Ineffective for Carbamate poisoning.

dose_calc.note: Must be given within 24h. Not effective if >48h or for Carbamate poisoning.

steps.text: Benzodiazepines for seizures: Diazepam 10mg IV (avoid Phenytoin in OPC)
steps.details: OPC-induced seizures: Diazepam IV. Phenytoin is less effective. Phenobarbital as second line.

steps.text: Monitor for intermediate syndrome (OPC): Day 2-4, proximal limb weakness, respiratory failure
steps.details: Intermediate syndrome occurs 24-96 hours after acute OPC poisoning. Watch for: neck flexion weakness, proximal limb weakness, respiratory failure. Patient may have appeared to improve then decompensate.

steps.text: ALUMINIUM PHOSPHIDE: NO antidote — supportive care, gastric lavage with KMnO4/NaHCO3
steps.details: Celphos/Quickphos (grain preservative) releases PH3 (phosphine gas) when exposed to moisture. Rapidly fatal. IV lipids (intralipid) has been tried. Circulatory support.

medications.name: Atropine Sulfate
medications.dose: 2-4mg IV q5-10 min until secretions dry (may need 10-100mg+)
medications.note: Endpoint = dry secretions, NOT heart rate. No ceiling dose in severe OPC.

medications.name: Pralidoxime (2-PAM)
medications.note: Must give within 24h. Not for Carbamates. Dilute in 100mL NS.

medications.name: Activated Charcoal
medications.note: Only within 1-2h, airway protected. No use in caustic ingestion.

medications.name: Diazepam (Seizures)
medications.note: For OPC-induced seizures. Phenytoin less effective in this context.

medications.name: Glycopyrrolate
medications.note: Does not cross blood-brain barrier — less CNS effects than Atropine

red_flags: SLUDGE + bronchospasm with secretions (severe OPC — high-dose atropine)
Respiratory failure (intubation + high-dose atropine)
Aluminium phosphide ingestion (universally fatal without ICU)
Delayed intermediate syndrome on day 2-4 (watch for proximal weakness)
GCS <8 with vomiting (aspiration risk — immediate airway securing)
pre_referral: Vital signs documented with exact atropine doses and times
referral_indications: Suspected Aluminium Phosphide (ICU for circulatory support)
Any ingestion requiring antidote not available locally

protocols.name: Cardiac Arrest (BLS/ACLS)
protocols.summary: CPR protocol: high-quality compressions, early defibrillation (shockable rhythms), epinephrine every 3-5 minutes, and reverse reversible causes.

timers.label: CPR Cycle (2 minutes)
timers.description: 2 minutes CPR before rhythm check

timers.label: Epinephrine Interval
timers.description: Next epinephrine dose (every 3-5 minutes)

timers.label: Compressor Switch
timers.description: Switch compressor every 2 min to prevent fatigue

steps.text: Confirm cardiac arrest: unresponsive, no normal breathing, no pulse (<10 sec check)
steps.details: Do not delay CPR for prolonged pulse check. If in doubt: START CPR.

steps.text: Call for help, call Code, activate emergency response team
steps.details: Designate roles: Team leader, compressor, airway, IV access, recorder.

steps.text: BEGIN CPR: 30 compressions : 2 breaths (30:2 ratio)
steps.details: Rate: 100-120/min. Depth: 5-6cm (2-2.4 inches). Allow FULL chest recoil. Minimize interruptions (<10 seconds). SWITCH COMPRESSOR every 2 min.

steps.text: Attach AED/Defibrillator — analyze rhythm IMMEDIATELY
steps.details: Minimize hands-off time. Continue CPR while defibrillator charging.

steps.text: SHOCKABLE RHYTHM (VF/pVT): Defibrillate 200J biphasic (360J monophasic)
steps.details: Deliver shock, IMMEDIATELY resume CPR for 2 minutes without rhythm check. START CPR CYCLE TIMER.

steps.text: IV/IO access: Epinephrine 1mg IV q3-5 min
steps.details: First epinephrine: AFTER 1st defibrillation (shockable) or immediately (non-shockable). START EPINEPHRINE INTERVAL TIMER.

dose_calc.note: 1mg IV = 10mL of 1:10,000 (0.1mg/mL). Child: 0.01mg/kg IV. Repeat every 3-5 min.

steps.text: Airway: bag-mask ventilation; intubate when available provider present (do not stop CPR)
steps.details: 8-10 breaths/min after intubation (do NOT interrupt chest compressions). Waveform capnography to confirm placement and monitor CPR quality (EtCO2 >10 = good CPR).

steps.text: After 3rd shock (VF/pVT): Amiodarone 300mg IV bolus
steps.details: Amiodarone 300mg IV in 20mL 5% dextrose. Second dose: 150mg IV if refractory. Lidocaine is an alternative (1-1.5mg/kg IV).

steps.text: NON-SHOCKABLE RHYTHM (Asystole/PEA): CPR + Epinephrine immediately
steps.details: No shock for asystole/PEA. Epinephrine 1mg IV immediately. Search for reversible causes. Continue 2-min CPR cycles.

steps.text: SEARCH AND TREAT reversible causes: 4H + 4T
steps.details: 4H: Hypoxia (suction/airway), Hypovolemia (IV fluids), H+ (acidosis — NaHCO3 if pH<7.1), Hypo/Hyperkalemia (check electrolytes). 4T: Tension pneumothorax (needle decompression), Tamponade (pericardiocentesis), Toxins (antidotes), Thrombosis — PE (consider thrombolytics) or MI (cath lab).

steps.text: Post-ROSC care: Targeted Temperature Management if remains unconscious
steps.details: Return of Spontaneous Circulation (ROSC): treat hypotension (MAP >65), oxygen (SpO2 94-98%), blood glucose 140-180mg/dL. Targeted temperature management 32-36°C for 24h if available.

medications.name: Epinephrine (Adrenaline) 1:10,000
medications.note: 1mg = 10mL of 1:10,000. First dose ASAP in non-shockable, after 1st shock in shockable

medications.name: Amiodarone
medications.dose: 300mg IV bolus after 3rd shock; 150mg for 2nd dose
medications.note: For refractory VF/pVT. Dilute in 20mL D5W. Only for shockable rhythms.

medications.name: Lidocaine (Lignocaine)
medications.note: Alternative to Amiodarone if unavailable

medications.name: Sodium Bicarbonate
medications.note: Only if pH <7.1, hyperkalemia, or TCA toxicity. Not routine.

medications.name: Calcium Chloride / Gluconate
medications.note: For hyperkalemia, hypocalcemia, calcium channel blocker toxicity

red_flags: EtCO2 <10mmHg despite CPR = poor prognosis / inadequate CPR quality
Signs of tension pneumothorax: absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation (needle decompress immediately)
Prolonged arrest with non-shockable rhythm and no reversible cause (consider stopping)
pre_referral: ROSC achieved with stable vital signs
Document total arrest time, interventions, medications with timing
Post-ROSC blood glucose and electrolytes checked
12-lead ECG for STEMI (activate cath lab if identified)
referral_indications: Suspected PE as cause (thrombolysis or ECMO-assisted PCI)
